sections.paragraph:
const. צְבָא plur. צְבָאֹו m. (but twice however, with a fem. verb, Isa 40:2 Dan 8:12).

(1) army, host, στρατός (pr. going forth to war), 2Sa 8:16 10:7 , and frequently. שַׂר הַצָּבָא leader of an army, Gen 21:22. אַנְשֵׁי הַצָּבָא soldiers, Num. 31:53 . יָצָא בַצָּבָה Deu 24:5. יָצָא לַצָּבָא Num 31:27, 28 Num 31:28and what is of far more frequent occurrence יָצָא צָבָא Num 1:3, seqq., to go out to the host, i.e. to make war. Often also used of the (sacred) host of the Levites, Num 4:23, 35 , 39 , 43 .

Specially-(a) צְבָא הַשָׁמַיִם the host of heaven is applied-(α) to the host of angels that stand round the throne of God (στρατιὰ οὐράνιος, Luk 2:13), 1Ki 22:19 2Ch 18:18 Psa 148:2 comp. צִבְאֵי יְיָ Psa 103:21 and שַׂר צְבָא יְיָ Jos 5:14, 15 Jos 5:15used of Jehovah himself [manifested in the person of the Son].-(β) used of the sun, moon, and stars (δυνάμεις τῶν οὐρανῶν, Matth. 24:29 ), Isa 34:4 40:26 45:12 Jerem. 33:22 Dan 8:10 often used when the worship of the stars is mentioned, Deu 4:19 17:3 2Ki 17:16 21:3 , 2Ki 21:5 Zep 1:5 it is sometimes so used that a special mention precedes of the sun and moon ( Deu 17:3 Jer 8:2), and even of the stars ( Deu 4:19 Dan 8:10); and this word is added as being more general, and comprehending all the heavenly bodies, as it were all the deities [?] of heaven ( Dan 4:32); compare Job 38:7 (where angels and stars are mentioned together); and Isa 24:21 (where the host of heaven, צְבָא הַמָּרֹום, is in opposition to the kings of the earth). Once rather more boldly צָבָא (by zeugma) is applied also to the inhabitants of the earth, or rather to whatever fills the earth (elsewhere מְלֹא הָאָרֶץ), the plants even being included. Gen 2:1, וַיְכֻלּוּ הַשָׁמַיִם וְהָאָרֶץ וְכָל־צְבָאָם, which, with the zeugma resolved, is thus explained in Neh 9:6, הַשָׁמַיִם וְכָל צְבָאָם הָאָרֶץ וְכָל־אֲשֶׁר עָלֶיהָ; compare Exo 20:11, הַשָׁמַיִם וְהָאָלֶץ … וְכָל אֲשֶׁר בָּם. Hence Jehovah is very often called-

(b) אֱלֹהִים צְבָאֹו Psa 80:15 יְהֹוָה אֱלֹהֵי צְבָאֹו Jer 5:14 15:16 38:17 44:7 יְהֹוָה אֱלֹהִים צְבָאֹו Psa 59:6 80:5 and יְהֹוָה צְבָאֹו “Jehovah (God) of the heavenly hosts” (a construction which I have noticed on Isa 1:9); this appellation of the most high God, is very frequent in the prophetical books, especially in Isaiah, Jeremiah, Zechariah, Malachi; never found in the Pentateuch [nor in Joshua] nor Judges [nor in Ezekiel, Job, or Solomon]. As to the meaning of the phrase, compare Jos 5:14, 15 Jos 5:15although the Hebrew writers seem sometimes to have regarded God as the leader and patron of the host of Israel [as of course he was], see 1Sa 17:45 compare 2Sa 5:24. In the later books of the Old Test. God is called, in the same sense אֱלֹהֵי 0ָ91השָׁמַיִם, אֱלָהּ שְׁמַיָּא; see שָׁמַיִם . LXX. commonly παντοκράτωρ.

(2) warfare, almost always figuratively used of a wretched and miserable condition, Job 7:1 10:17 14:14 Isa 40:2 Dan 10:1, “and the edict is true וְצָבָא גָרֹול and (belongs to) a long warfare,” to many calamities to be endured.
